main_summary.summary: Clifford D. Browner Jr., born 1946 in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia, recounts coming of age in southwest Georgia during the Albany Movement's expansion into rural Terrell County in 1962-1963. Interviewed by Hasan Kwame Jeffries on March 9, 2013 at Albany State University, Browner describes his father's sharecropping background and later work at a fertilizer plant in Albany, his mother's domestic labor and lunchroom cook positions, and the family's Dunbar Elementary and George Washington Carver High School education in segregated Terrell County. As a Carver High senior, Browner and classmate Clovis Jones wrote a letter to the Terrell County school superintendent demanding new textbooks rather than hand-me-downs from white schools, drawing reprisal from principal E.E.S.S. and forcing Browner's resignation as senior class president. He recalls the burning of Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Sasser after it hosted SNCC mass meetings led by Charles Sherrod, the intimidation visits by Sheriff Z.T. Matthews and armed white men, his August 1963 train trip to the March on Washington funded by his church-rebuilding wages, and his subsequent Army service at Fort Knox and Kitzingen, Germany. The interview closes with Browner's reflections on perceived versus actual freedom in the North after his years working at GM plants in Flint, Michigan and Doraville, Georgia.
key_themes: Albany Movement in rural Terrell County
Family economic survival under Jim Crow
main_summary.historical_significance: Browner's testimony documents the Albany Movement's rural Terrell County extension from a foot-soldier teenager's perspective: how SNCC mass meetings migrated from Mt. Zion Baptist Church near Dawson to Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Sasser because that was where local attendance was concentrated, and how the church was burned shortly after. His firsthand account of Sheriff Z.T. Matthews's armed-white-men intimidation visit to a mass meeting, of Charles Sherrod's presence that night, and of the church-rebuilding labor that paid him $1.40 an hour and funded his trip to the March on Washington provides ground-level texture to a county that historians more often discuss through SNCC organizers' perspectives. The textbook-letter incident with Clovis Jones documents in-school Black student protest in 1963-1964 Terrell County and the costs imposed by a Black principal acting under superintendent pressure. Browner's closing reflection on perceived versus structural freedom — North versus South, racial separation versus economic separation, the tax-base hollowing of cities with Black leadership — extends the interview's value beyond movement chronology into Black working-class analysis of post-1965 conditions.

chapters.title: Family Origins in Terrell County
chapters.summary: Browner introduces his birth in Sasser, Georgia, his parents Clifford Sr. (from Webster County) and Johnny Lou (from Terrell County), and the family courtship story: his mother praying at the woodpile after her own mother's death, then meeting his father at school the next day. He describes his father's transition from sharecropping to work at a fertilizer plant in Albany.
related_events: family migration from farm to Albany wage labor

chapters.title: Segregated Childhood and Community
chapters.summary: Browner describes his father's shielding of the family from direct racial confrontation in Albany, the all-Black community of dirt streets and three-room houses in which he grew up, and the lessons of deference in front of whites picked up at home and through observation. He recounts that his parents raised four children plus an extended-family cousin in their household.

chapters.title: Dunbar and Carver Schools
chapters.summary: Browner attended Dunbar Elementary School — a faded white wooden building with a pot-bellied stove, no indoor plumbing, and outdoor coal-fetching duties — until 1955 when a new building was constructed. He then transferred to George Washington Carver High School, seven miles away by bus from Sasser. His mother worked first in the white elementary school cafeteria in Sasser, then in the Dunbar lunchroom where she eventually became head cook.

chapters.title: Movement Comes to Sasser
chapters.summary: Browner first learned about the Albany Movement through his father and older sister, who were active. Mass meetings had been held at Mt. Zion Baptist Church near Dawson but moved to Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Sasser to reach the population center. Browner attended the meetings, where SNCC organizers reported on voter registration drives, beatings, arrests, and confrontations with the Sheriff's Department in Terrell, Lee, and Dougherty counties.
related_events: SNCC voter registration in southwest Georgia

chapters.title: The Textbook Letter
chapters.summary: Browner and classmate Clovis Jones wrote a letter to the Terrell County school superintendent requesting new books because Black students were receiving hand-me-downs with missing pages and written-in notes. The principal, E.E.S.S., called Browner and Jones into his office, said they should be expelled, and they were subsequently surveilled at recess. Browner was forced to resign as senior class president for his role in the letter.
related_events: Carver High School textbook letter incident

chapters.title: Mt. Olive Church Burned
chapters.summary: Browner recounts Sheriff Z.T. Matthews and other armed white men, dressed in regular clothes but carrying pistols, walking into a mass meeting at Mt. Olive Baptist Church to intimidate attendees. SNCC organizer Charles Sherrod was present that night. The church was burned to the ground later, and Browner went with his father to see the smoldering remains. He recalls rumors that whites discussed burning out his family's house but spared it because elderly non-involved neighbors lived nearby.

chapters.title: March on Washington 1963
chapters.summary: Browner traveled by train from Albany to Washington, D.C. for the August 1963 March on Washington, his first time away from his mother's home overnight. He had earned the spending money rebuilding burned churches at $1.40 an hour. He recalls arriving in DC underdressed for the colder weather, hearing Dr. King's speech, seeing Peter, Paul and Mary perform, and noticing John Lewis. He took the train home the same night.

chapters.title: Army Service and Germany
chapters.summary: Unable to afford college, Browner enlisted in July 1964, completed basic training at Fort Gordon, Georgia, advanced communications training at Fort Knox, Kentucky, and was stationed at Hubbard Barracks in Kitzingen, West Germany for 26 months. He describes the army as his first integrated environment, traveling briefly to Frankfurt and Hamburg, being introduced to jazz by Black servicemen from northern cities, and being mentored by an older white squad leader who compared Browner favorably to his own 17-year-old brother.

chapters.title: Return Home and Albany State
chapters.summary: Browner returned home and was shocked that little had changed economically despite legal integration. He enrolled at Albany State College because Tennessee State would not start him until January. He majored in sociology while working two part-time jobs, lived first at home then in an apartment. He briefly visited his father at the Albany fertilizer plant where dust coated the workers.

chapters.title: Reidsville and Flint
chapters.summary: Browner took a counselor job in an ex-offender program at Reidsville State Prison, recruited by the Georgia labor department, but the warden — uncomfortable with Browner being the first Black non-maintenance employee there — promised to get rid of him. Browner soon left for Flint, Michigan to join boyhood friend Doug Settle. He worked at the Chevrolet Sheffield Truck Assembly Plant, then in computer operations, then sold insurance for Equitable Life Insurance Company.

chapters.title: Doraville and Forced Retirement
chapters.summary: Browner transferred back to General Motors at the Doraville plant north of Atlanta in DeKalb County, Georgia, working there for 20 years until a head-on highway collision forced his retirement. He returned to Terrell County and observed that white farmers had shifted from sharecropping to business farming with tractors and combines after running off the Black tenants who had asked to register to vote.

chapters.title: North and South Reflections
chapters.summary: Browner closes with reflection on the difference between Southern and Northern racism — the South's explicit position-knowing versus the North's perceived freedom that masks structural separation. He observes that gains since the movement appear surface-level: tax base flight to suburbs has hollowed out cities with Black mayors, replacing racial separation with economic separation while leaving Black political leaders blamed for conditions they have no resources to change.

loc_video.caption: Clifford Browner oral history interview conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
